people who interview you usually don't know what to ask, as they don't know their own concerns

so it's up to you to figure all of them out and make sure all are adressed. big hack is straight up sharing your screen and showing a demo that they did not ask for

if you're joining a startup, add a feature to their website that shows high taste and agency. if you're joining a lab, build some small ml project that is relevant and explain some smart decisions you made etc
this doesn't work when a randomly selected software engineer that won't be on your team and that has no stake in you being hired is interviewing you btw, which is apparently common

when interviewing for jobs, don't talk as if they're doing you a favor by hiring you and giving you money

focus on removing 100% of unknowns from the interviewer, even if they don't ask you; do you work hard, learn fast, have you done this before, show something live
idk why this mindset exists and why it's so universal. something with how schools "teach" us how to get a job makes us do this

"companies want to make money, so show them how you can make them money" just keep this in mind. discard whatever school told you about getting a job, assume above, and extrapolate all your actions from that

New JavaScript developer challenge: a three-slot array, a .map() call, and a counter. Seems straightforward, but this code may catch you off guard. Share your answer in the comments.
Not every array slot behaves like a real element. Under what conditions would an array method skip a slot?

The result is 0. new Array(3) creates an array with three empty slots rather than three actual elements. Many array methods, including map, skip empty slots. Because the callback never runs, the counter never increments.

Most teams don’t hit a fine-tuning bottleneck because they picked the wrong algorithm.

They hit the bottleneck because the loop around training is too slow:
-data plumbing
-eval setup
-deployment friction
-too much guesswork between runs

Also: fine-tuning is not one tool.

Use SFT when you have strong demos.
Use RFT when the reward is easier to measure than the ideal answer.
Use DPO when preferences are the signal.

Serious teams use all 3.

The destination isn’t a fine-tuned model sitting on a shelf.

It’s a domain-specific agent that keeps getting better as the loop gets tighter.
